ctx: acquire /dev/sdb and record an integrity hash
code: dd if=/dev/sdb of=evidence.dd bs=4096
# ready for analysis
why: Without hashing the source and the image, you cannot prove the copy was not altered.
fixWhy: Hash both the source and the image (SHA-256) and compare; matching hashes prove integrity.

ctx: create a court-admissible image of /dev/sdc
code: dd if=/dev/sdc of=evidence.dd bs=4096
# imaging done - but is it verifiable?
why: Plain dd computes no hash, so the image cannot be proven unaltered for court.
fixWhy: dc3dd with hash=sha256 hashes during acquisition; ewfacquire (E01) is another forensic option.

ctx: on compromised WS-014: what do you collect first
code: image the WS-014 disk first, then capture RAM
(RAM is far more volatile than disk)
why: The order of volatility says collect what disappears first.
fixWhy: Order of volatility: CPU/cache -> RAM -> network state -> disk -> archives. RAM dies on power-off.

ctx: create a court-admissible image of /dev/nvme0n1
code: dd if=/dev/nvme0n1 of=evidence.dd bs=4096
# imaging done - but is it verifiable?
why: Plain dd computes no hash, so the image cannot be proven unaltered for court.
fixWhy: dc3dd with hash=sha256 hashes during acquisition; ewfacquire (E01) is another forensic option.

ctx: build a super-timeline from the image C.vhdx
code: psort.py -o l2tcsv -w timeline.csv timeline.plaso
# ...but timeline.plaso has not been created yet
why: psort.py only PROCESSES an existing plaso storage file; nothing has extracted the events yet.
fixWhy: log2timeline.py <storage> <source> extracts events into the plaso file; psort.py then formats it.

ctx: image /dev/nvme0n1 and prove the copy is intact
code: dd if=/dev/nvme0n1 of=evidence.dd bs=4096
# ready for analysis
why: Without hashing the source and the image, you cannot prove the copy was not altered.
fixWhy: Hash both the source and the image (SHA-256) and compare; matching hashes prove integrity.

ctx: find execution evidence for dropper.exe
code: MFTECmd.exe -f C:\$MFT --csv C:\analysis
# trying to prove dropper.exe was executed
why: The $MFT records file creation/modification, not execution; it cannot prove a program ran.
fixWhy: Prefetch (parsed by PECmd) holds run count + last-run times - the execution evidence; also ShimCache/AmCache.

ctx: recover the malware's C2 connections from WEB-SRV's memory
code: vol.py -f host.lime windows.pslist
# goal: find the malware's C2 network connections
why: windows.pslist lists processes, not connections, so it cannot reveal the C2 endpoints.
fixWhy: windows.netscan recovers TCP/UDP endpoints and their owning PIDs from the memory image.

ctx: recover the malware's C2 connections from WS-05's memory
code: vol.py -f dump.mem windows.pslist
# goal: find the malware's C2 network connections
why: windows.pslist lists processes, not connections, so it cannot reveal the C2 endpoints.
fixWhy: windows.netscan recovers TCP/UDP endpoints and their owning PIDs from the memory image.

ctx: extract timeline events from /evidence/ into a plaso file
code: psort.py -o l2tcsv -w timeline.csv timeline.plaso
# ...but timeline.plaso has not been created yet
why: psort.py only PROCESSES an existing plaso storage file; nothing has extracted the events yet.
fixWhy: log2timeline.py <storage> <source> extracts events into the plaso file; psort.py then formats it.

ctx: find execution evidence for mimikatz.exe
code: MFTECmd.exe -f C:\$MFT --csv C:\analysis
# trying to prove mimikatz.exe was executed
why: The $MFT records file creation/modification, not execution; it cannot prove a program ran.
fixWhy: Prefetch (parsed by PECmd) holds run count + last-run times - the execution evidence; also ShimCache/AmCache.

ctx: list processes from host.lime, including unlinked ones
code: vol.py -f host.lime windows.pslist
# a rootkit may have unlinked its process to hide
why: windows.pslist walks the active process list, which a rootkit can unlink from to stay hidden.
fixWhy: windows.psscan pool-scans memory for EPROCESS structures, revealing unlinked/hidden processes (DKOM).

code: vol.py -f memory.dmp windows.pslist
# a rootkit may have unlinked its process to hide

ctx: find network connections in the dump mem.raw
code: vol.py -f mem.raw windows.pslist
# goal: find the malware's C2 network connections
why: windows.pslist lists processes, not connections, so it cannot reveal the C2 endpoints.
fixWhy: windows.netscan recovers TCP/UDP endpoints and their owning PIDs from the memory image.

fixWhy: windows.netscan recovers TCP/UDP endpoints and their owning PIDs from the memory image.

ctx: search Windows-PowerShell.evtx for a specific Event ID
code: grep 4625 Windows-PowerShell.evtx
# .evtx is a binary event-log file
why: EVTX is a binary format; grep cannot parse it and will miss or garble events.
fixWhy: Get-WinEvent parses EVTX and filters by Event ID (4625 = failed logon); EvtxECmd --csv is the Zimmerman alternative.
